section.title: Sing a New Song for His Marvels
section.sectionRationale: Closely echoing the exodus victory song and Isaiah's promises of salvation reaching the ends of the earth, this enthronement psalm summons voices, instruments, and all creation, sea, rivers, and mountains alike, to celebrate the LORD's saving victory and His coming to judge the world in righteousness.
section.thesis: Because the LORD's own right hand and holy arm have worked salvation seen by all the ends of the earth, all the earth, and even creation itself, is summoned to a new song of joyful, musical praise as He comes to judge the world in righteousness.
section.frameNote: Three movements: a new song for the LORD's saving victory made known to the nations, verses 1-3; a summons to loud, musical praise before the King, verses 4-6; and creation's joyful chorus as the LORD comes to judge the earth, verses 7-9.

units.label: He has done wonders
units.note: a new song for the Lord's saving victory

verses.text: A Psalm. Sing to the LORD a {{chadash|new}} song, for He has done wonders; His right hand and holy arm have gained Him the victory.
The LORD has proclaimed His salvation and revealed His righteousness to the nations.
He has remembered His love and faithfulness to the house of Israel; all the ends of the earth have seen the salvation of our God.

units.label: Make a joyful noise before the King
units.note: a summons to loud, musical praise

verses.text: Make a joyful noise to the LORD, all the earth; break forth, let your cry ring out, and sing praises!
Sing praises to the LORD with the lyre, in melodious song with the harp.
With trumpets and the blast of the ram's horn shout for joy before the LORD, the King.

units.label: He comes to judge the earth

verses.text: Let the sea resound, and all that fills it, the world, and all who dwell in it.
Let the rivers clap their hands, let the mountains sing together for joy
before the LORD, for He comes to {{shaphat|judge}} the earth. He will judge the world with righteousness and the peoples with equity.

chadash.gloss: New, fresh. A new song answers a new act of God's salvation; Revelation 5:9 and 14:3 picture the redeemed singing just such a new song before God's throne.

shaphat.gloss: To judge, govern, or vindicate. The LORD's coming to judge the earth is not merely dreaded punishment but longed-for justice, the same role the New Testament assigns to Christ at His return, Acts 17:31.

basic.context: This psalm calls for a new song because the LORD's own right hand and holy arm have worked a visible salvation, seen by all the ends of the earth. Voices, harps, trumpets, and even the sea, rivers, and mountains are summoned to celebrate His victory and His coming to judge the world in righteousness.
basic.hermeneutics: The specific historical deliverance behind the psalm is not named, it may recall the exodus, the conquest, or deliverance from exile, but the psalm applies its call to sing a fresh song to every fresh act of God's salvation, and ultimately to the greatest salvation of all in Christ.
basic.originalLanguages: A chadash, new, song is called for because the LORD's arm has worked visible salvation, seen by all the ends of the earth. His coming is to shaphat, judge or govern rightly, the world, a judgment longed for as vindication of the oppressed rather than merely feared.
basic.typology: The 'new song' of verse 1 anticipates the new song sung by the redeemed before God's throne, Revelation 5:9 and 14:3; the LORD's 'right hand and holy arm' working salvation anticipates the outstretched, wounded arms of Christ on the cross, through whom salvation was finally and fully worked.
basic.christ: This psalm's picture of salvation worked by the LORD's own right hand and holy arm, made known to the nations and seen by the ends of the earth, is fulfilled when Simeon holds the infant Jesus and calls Him God's salvation, 'a light for revelation to the Gentiles,' Luke 2:30-32, and the psalm's coming Judge is identified with Christ, appointed by God to judge the living and the dead, Acts 10:42.
basic.god: God is the mighty Deliverer whose own strength, not human armies, works salvation, faithful to His covenant love toward Israel while revealing His righteousness to every nation, and the coming Judge whose righteous rule sets even creation itself singing for joy.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 15:6 celebrates the LORD's right hand glorious in power, the same image this psalm uses of salvation. Luke 2:30-32 shows Simeon naming the infant Jesus as God's salvation revealed to the nations. Revelation 5:9 and 14:3 picture the redeemed singing a new song. Acts 17:31 fixes a day when God will judge the world through the Man He has appointed.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's new song for salvation worked by the LORD's own arm, made known to every nation, and its hope of a coming righteous Judge, finds its full answer in Christ, God's salvation revealed to the Gentiles and the appointed Judge of the living and the dead.

context.literary: One of the 'orphan' psalms without a named author, this psalm shares striking language with the exodus victory song, Exodus 15:1-18, and Isaiah's promises of salvation reaching the nations, structured in three stanzas moving from a new song for salvation, to musical praise, to all creation's response to the coming Judge.
context.historical: Grouped among the enthronement psalms of Book IV, likely used at festivals such as Tabernacles, this psalm's confidence that 'all the ends of the earth have seen the salvation of our God' would have reassured a post-exilic community that God's saving power was not confined to a single victory in the past.
context.canonical: Follows directly after Psalm 97's storm theophany and precedes Psalm 99's call to holiness, forming part of a sustained sequence celebrating the LORD's kingship before the personal lament of Psalm 102, and closely echoing both the exodus song and Isaiah 42:10-17.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To call God's people, and through them the whole earth, to fresh, exuberant, musical praise because the LORD's own strength has worked a visible salvation known to every nation, and to anticipate His coming to judge the world in perfect righteousness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific, though unnamed, act of God's salvation witnessed by the nations; it prescribes for every generation a pattern of fresh song and full-voiced, instrumental worship whenever God acts to save, and confident hope in His coming righteous judgment.

debates.question: What specific historical deliverance, if any, lies behind this psalm's celebration of salvation in verses 1-3?

views.label: It recalls the exodus from Egypt
views.case: The language of God's right hand and arm working salvation closely echoes Exodus 15:6, suggesting the exodus as the founding memory behind the psalm's praise.

views.label: It recalls Israel's return from Babylonian exile
views.case: The psalm's placement in Book IV, its emphasis that 'all the ends of the earth' have seen this salvation, and its parallels with Isaiah's exilic promises fit a post-exilic setting celebrating deliverance from Babylon.

debates.bearing: Either deliverance, or both together, illustrates the pattern this psalm sets for every generation, that a real, historical act of God's salvation calls for genuinely fresh praise, a pattern completed in the salvation accomplished at the cross.

study.originalLanguages: The chadash, new, song of verse 1 answers a fresh act of God's salvation, worked by His own right hand and holy arm. His coming to shaphat, judge, the earth in verse 9 is a longed-for act of righteous governance and vindication, not mere condemnation.

typology.type: The new song sung for the LORD's saving victory, verse 1
typology.fulfillment: Revelation 5:9 and 14:3 picture the redeemed of every nation singing a new song before God's throne and the Lamb.

typology.type: The LORD's right hand and holy arm working salvation, verse 1
typology.fulfillment: The salvation worked by God's own arm anticipates the outstretched arms of Christ on the cross, and Simeon's naming of Jesus as God's salvation revealed to the nations, Luke 2:30-32.

study.christ: Simeon takes up language like this psalm's when he holds the infant Jesus and calls Him God's salvation, prepared in the sight of all peoples, 'a light for revelation to the Gentiles,' Luke 2:30-32, directly fulfilling this psalm's hope that 'all the ends of the earth have seen the salvation of our God.' And the coming Judge of verse 9 is identified in Acts 10:42 and 17:31 as the risen Christ, appointed by God to judge the living and the dead.
study.god: God is the mighty Deliverer whose own right hand and holy arm work salvation, not merely a distant observer of human affairs. He is faithful to His covenant love toward Israel even while revealing His righteousness to every nation, and He is the coming Judge whose righteous rule sets creation itself, sea, rivers, and mountains, singing for joy.

crossReferences.note: The exodus victory song celebrates the LORD's right hand glorious in power, the same image this psalm uses of salvation worked by God's own arm.
Simeon names the infant Jesus as God's salvation, prepared in the sight of all peoples, fulfilling this psalm's hope that the ends of the earth would see God's salvation.
Pictures the redeemed from every nation singing a new song before God's throne and the Lamb, answering this psalm's opening call.
Identifies the risen Christ as the one appointed by God to judge the living and the dead, fulfilling this psalm's coming Judge.

study.oneStory: This psalm's new song for salvation worked by the LORD's own arm, witnessed by every nation, and its hope for a coming righteous Judge, run forward to Christ, in whom God's salvation was revealed to all peoples and who will return as the appointed Judge of the whole earth.

quiz.q: What has caused the LORD to be praised with a new song in verses 1-3?

options.text: His right hand and holy arm have worked a visible salvation, seen by all the ends of the earth.
options.note: Verses 1-3 credit God's own arm with a salvation made known to the nations and seen by the whole earth.

options.text: A new king has just been crowned in Jerusalem.
options.note: The psalm credits the LORD's own salvation, not a human coronation, as the cause for the new song.

options.text: The temple has just been rebuilt from its foundations.
options.note: Temple rebuilding is not the stated cause; the psalm specifically credits God's own saving action.

options.text: A new harvest has produced record grain yields.
options.note: The psalm's praise is for salvation and righteousness revealed, not agricultural abundance.

quiz.q: What instruments does the psalm name for musical praise in verses 5-6?

options.text: The lyre, the harp, trumpets, and the ram's horn.
options.note: Verses 5-6 specifically name the lyre, harp, trumpets, and ram's horn as instruments of praise.

options.text: Only hand clapping, with no instruments at all.
options.note: Rivers 'clap their hands' later as a poetic image, but verses 5-6 specifically name stringed and wind instruments.

options.text: Drums and cymbals exclusively.
options.note: The psalm names the lyre, harp, trumpets, and ram's horn, not drums and cymbals.

options.text: Bells worn on the priests' garments.
options.note: Priestly bells are not mentioned here; the named instruments are the lyre, harp, trumpets, and ram's horn.

quiz.q: How does creation respond in verses 7-8?

options.text: The sea resounds, the rivers clap their hands, and the mountains sing together for joy.
options.note: These verses personify the sea, rivers, and mountains joining in joyful praise before the LORD.

options.text: Creation falls completely silent in fear.
options.note: The psalm describes creation resounding and singing for joy, not falling silent.

options.text: Only human worshipers respond; creation is unaffected.
options.note: The psalm explicitly personifies the sea, rivers, and mountains as responding, not only human worshipers.

options.text: The mountains crumble in judgment.
options.note: The mountains are pictured singing for joy, not crumbling in judgment, in this psalm.

quiz.q: Why does the psalm say creation rejoices in verse 9?

options.text: Because the LORD comes to judge the earth with righteousness and the peoples with equity.
options.note: Verse 9 explicitly grounds creation's joy in the LORD's coming righteous and equitable judgment.

options.text: Because a military alliance has been formed among the nations.
options.note: The stated reason is the LORD's righteous judgment, not any human political alliance.

options.text: Because the psalmist has composed a technically skillful poem.
options.note: The joy is grounded in God's coming righteous judgment, not the psalmist's poetic skill.

options.text: Because the harvest festival has concluded successfully.
options.note: The text specifically credits the LORD's coming judgment as the reason for creation's joy, not a festival's conclusion.

quiz.q: Who does the New Testament identify as fulfilling this psalm's hope that the ends of the earth would see God's salvation?

options.text: Jesus Christ, whom Simeon calls God's salvation and a light for revelation to the Gentiles.
options.note: Luke 2:30-32 records Simeon applying language like this psalm's directly to the infant Jesus.

options.text: The Roman Empire's expansion across the known world.
options.note: The New Testament identifies Christ, not any human empire, as the fulfillment of this hope.

options.text: The prophet Isaiah alone, with no further New Testament fulfillment.
options.note: Isaiah's prophecies anticipate this salvation, but Luke shows its fulfillment in the person of Jesus.

options.text: King Solomon's temple building projects.
options.note: Solomon's temple is not identified in the New Testament as the fulfillment of this psalm's hope for global salvation.

questions: The psalm calls for a 'new song' because of a fresh, visible act of God's salvation. What specific thing has God done for you recently that deserves its own fresh song of praise, rather than routine words?

This psalm envisions loud, full-voiced, instrumental praise, not quiet or restrained worship. Where might you be holding back in expressing joy and gratitude to God that this psalm would call you to release?
Even the sea, rivers, and mountains are pictured joining the praise. How does remembering that all creation is designed to glorify God shape the way you notice the world around you this week?

This psalm's hope, that the ends of the earth would see God's salvation, is fulfilled in Christ, revealed to the Gentiles. Who in your life still needs to hear and see that salvation made known through you?

Creation rejoices specifically because the LORD is coming to judge the world in righteousness and equity. Where in your life or in the world around you do you most need to trust that God's righteous judgment is still coming?

translationNotes.note: The phrase 'His right hand and holy arm have gained Him the victory' closely echoes the exodus song, 'Your right hand, O LORD, is glorious in power,' Exodus 15:6.
The 'ram's horn' translates the Hebrew shophar, a curved trumpet used both in worship and to announce significant events such as the Year of Jubilee.
The psalm's closing hope that the LORD will 'judge the world with righteousness' is echoed almost verbatim at the close of Psalm 96:13, forming a refrain across this cluster of enthronement psalms.

meta.notes: The local scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error in this sandbox, so the BSB text was obtained via WebSearch and web_fetch from biblehub.com/bsb/psalms/98.htm, cross-checked against the section map's 9-verse bound. Footnote markers were stripped and the em dash in verse 4 of the source was converted to a comma; the superscription 'A Psalm.' was folded into verse 1 as printed by the source, keeping the verse count at 9. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) could be confirmed this session as preached specifically on Psalm 98; a Desiring God message on this psalm was found but by a different teacher, so status is 'none' rather than risk misattribution.
